12 min read
Just now

--

Every TLS certificate ever issued for your domain is permanently, publicly, searchably recorded. Every DNS resolution your infrastructure has ever made may be sitting in someone else’s database. The internet remembers everything — and the most valuable reconnaissance requires no contact with the target at all.

There is a question that arrives quietly, usually weeks after someone gets comfortable with network scanning. They have learned how ports work. They understand service detection. They can read scan results with confidence. They know that DNS reveals infrastructure maps for free. They feel equipped.
And then they discover that the most important information was available before they sent a single packet.
Not just DNS. Not just WHOIS. An entire world of passive intelligence — records that already exist, observations that have already been made, databases that are continuously maintained — all of it queryable, all of it public, and none of it requiring any contact with the target’s infrastructure.
This article covers two of the most powerful passive sources that sit alongside DNS: Certificate Transparency — the permanent record of every TLS certificate ever issued — and passive DNS databases — the historical film of how domains have resolved over time.
Together with DNS, these sources form the foundation of passive reconnaissance: building complete intelligence pictures without sending a single packet.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Reconnaissance Spectrum
Every technique you have encountered so far in this series was active. You sent packets to a target. Those packets crossed networks. They arrived at a destination. They triggered responses. Even the quietest SYN scan — the one that never completes a handshake — still sends a packet that arrives at someone else’s server.
Active reconnaissance is powerful. It is also expensive. Every packet you send is a tradeoff: intelligence gained versus detection risk incurred. Send more packets, learn more, but increase the chance that someone notices. Send fewer packets, stay invisible, but learn less.
There is, however, an entire category of intelligence gathering where you never send a single packet to the target.
This is passive reconnaissance.
And it is almost always underused.
Imagine you are investigating a company’s physical headquarters. Active reconnaissance is like walking up to the building, trying the doors, looking through windows, and noting what you see. You are physically present. Your visit is recorded on security cameras. Someone might notice you.
Passive reconnaissance is like sitting in a café across the street, reading the company’s public filings, checking their building permits, looking at satellite imagery on a mapping service, and reading employee reviews on job sites. You never approach the building. You never appear on any camera. But you might learn more than the person who walked up and tried the doors.
Both approaches produce intelligence. Only one leaves evidence.
The reconnaissance spectrum has four levels. Completely passive techniques require zero interaction with the target infrastructure. Semi-passive techniques involve minimal interaction — usually DNS queries to public resolvers. Active techniques send packets directly to the target. Intrusive techniques go further — vulnerability exploitation, authentication attempts, brute force.
Most beginners start at the active end. They receive a target and immediately begin scanning.
Experienced practitioners start at the passive end. They spend hours — sometimes days — collecting intelligence before sending a single packet. Not because they are afraid of being detected. Because passive intelligence makes active reconnaissance faster, more focused, and more accurate.
If you already know the target’s IP ranges from ASN records, you do not need to guess. If you already know their subdomains from Certificate Transparency logs, you do not need to brute-force. If you already know their mail infrastructure from DNS, you already understand part of their architecture.
Every piece of passive intelligence narrows the scope of active reconnaissance. Narrower scope means fewer packets. Fewer packets means less noise. Less noise means better assessments.
The previous article covered DNS — the richest passive source available at the protocol level. This article covers the sources that sit alongside DNS.

Certificate Transparency: The Permanent Record
In 2013, a Certificate Authority called DigiNotar was compromised. Attackers gained access to DigiNotar’s signing infrastructure and issued fraudulent TLS certificates for google.com — among other high-value domains. They used those certificates to intercept traffic, potentially surveilling users who believed their connections were secure.
The breach went undetected for weeks.
When it was finally discovered, the damage was catastrophic. DigiNotar’s entire trust was revoked. Browsers removed DigiNotar from their trusted certificate lists overnight. The company went bankrupt. But the deeper problem was not DigiNotar itself — it was the structural weakness the breach exposed.
Before Certificate Transparency, Certificate Authorities could issue certificates for any domain, and nobody had a systematic way to monitor it. Domain owners might discover a fraudulent certificate through careful log inspection — if they were lucky. Most would never know.
The response was Certificate Transparency — a public, append-only log of every TLS certificate issued by every Certificate Authority. The design is elegant: logs are cryptographically guaranteed to be append-only (entries cannot be deleted or modified without detection), publicly auditable (anyone can verify a log’s integrity), and distributed (multiple independent log servers maintain copies).
Starting in 2018, Chrome required all new certificates to be logged. Other browsers followed. Today, Certificate Transparency is effectively mandatory for any publicly trusted TLS certificate. If a Certificate Authority issues a certificate for your domain, that certificate appears in a public log whether you asked for it or not.
The security benefit is significant: domain owners can monitor logs for unauthorised certificates issued for their domains. If someone issues a certificate for your domain without your knowledge, you can detect it.
The reconnaissance benefit is equally significant — and rarely discussed with the same enthusiasm.
Every certificate logged in Certificate Transparency is a permanent public record. That record contains the domain name, any subdomains listed in the certificate (including Subject Alternative Names), the Certificate Authority that issued it, and the issuance and expiration dates.
For reconnaissance, this means something remarkable: every TLS certificate ever issued for a domain is permanently, publicly, searchably recorded.
If a company issued a certificate for internal-dev.meridian-tech.com three years ago — and later decommissioned that server — the certificate still exists in the transparency log. The subdomain is still discoverable. The naming convention is still visible. The infrastructure relationship is still documented.
The certificate was logged when it was issued. It stays logged forever.
Tools like crt.sh provide free, searchable access to Certificate Transparency logs. A single query for %.meridian-tech.com returns every certificate ever issued for any subdomain of that domain. The result is often dozens or hundreds of subdomains — many of which do not appear in current DNS because they have been decommissioned, renamed, or were internal-only systems that were accidentally issued public certificates.
This is the part that surprises people. You are not querying the target’s infrastructure. You are querying a third-party transparency log. The target has no way of knowing someone looked. The technique is entirely passive — as passive as reading a public filing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What Certificate Transparency reveals that DNS does not:
Subdomains that no longer resolve. A hostname that was decommissioned two years ago might still appear in a certificate issued in 2021. Current DNS will not show it. Certificate Transparency will.
Naming conventions. The pattern of subdomains reveals how the organisation names its infrastructure. app-prod-01, app-staging-02, app-dev-03 tells you the naming scheme. Once you know the scheme, you can predict other hostnames that might exist.
Infrastructure transitions. Certificates issued by one Certificate Authority in 2020, then a different one in 2023, suggest a migration. Certificates for the same hostname issued to different organisations suggest a domain or infrastructure transfer.
Certificate scope. A certificate listing ten subdomains in its Subject Alternative Names reveals relationships between systems that might not be obvious from DNS alone. app.meridian-tech.com and admin.meridian-tech.com sharing a certificate tells you they are served by the same server or load balancer.
Expired and revoked certificates. A certificate that expired months ago but was never renewed might indicate infrastructure that was abandoned without proper decommissioning. It still appears in the log.

The key insight is permanence. DNS records can be changed or deleted. Firewall rules can be tightened. Services can be taken offline. But Certificate Transparency entries are append-only. The record of what existed persists long after the infrastructure disappears.
For defenders, this means your Certificate Transparency history is a public record of every subdomain you have ever operated — including the ones you have forgotten about. Periodically reviewing your own CT entries is one of the simplest ways to discover shadow IT, orphaned infrastructure, and certificate misissuance.
For attackers, CT logs are a goldmine. They reveal the full scope of an organisation’s web-facing infrastructure — including systems that DNS no longer points to, that port scans will never find, and that the organisation’s own teams may have lost track of.

Passive DNS: Watching Without Participating
DNS is dynamic. Records change. IP addresses shift. Services migrate between providers. Domains are registered, used, and abandoned.
Standard DNS queries tell you what a domain resolves to right now. They tell you nothing about what it resolved to yesterday, last month, or three years ago.
Passive DNS databases fill that gap.
Security companies, DNS providers, and research organisations have been collecting DNS resolution data for years. Every time their infrastructure observes a DNS query and response — from malware analysis, from user browsing, from sensor networks, from security products — they record the observation. Over time, these collections become historical databases mapping the complete resolution history of millions of domains.
A passive DNS query for mail.meridian-tech.com might return a timeline:

• 2021: resolved to 198.51.100.12 — hosted on-premises.

• 2022: resolved to 203.0.113.45 — migrated to AWS.

• 2023: resolved to 192.0.2.88 — moved to Azure.

• 2024: resolved to current address — stabilised.
This history reveals infrastructure migrations, cloud provider transitions, and architectural evolution. The organisation moved from on-premises to AWS and then to Azure. That information affects your assessment strategy because each platform has different default security characteristics, different network architectures, and different common misconfigurations.
